caption.text: Yes, because there are UX principles that remain
universal, not just because we’re not dealing with the same situation.
Let me explain. On the web, the user is often sitting
in front of a wide screen and has time. On mobile, they’re often standing,
distracted, with one hand, and usually in a rush.
So when you open a mobile app, for example on the subway,
you only want to do one thing. And it’s not to explore or
understand the whole product,
it’s to act directly.
So the first tip is: you don’t design a mobile app as the
responsive version of a web app. On mobile,
what really matters
is not how rich the screens are, but how fast you get to the main
action. And a good mobile screen answers a single intention.
So if your screen offers too many choices,
too many actions, or too much information,
your user drops off.
The second tip is the thumb. On mobile,
everything important has to be easily and effortlessly accessible.
Whether it’s the main CTA,
the next action, or the confirmation.
If the user has to stretch their thumb or change their grip,
you lose comfort and therefore usage.
The third tip is sensory feedback.
On mobile, the experience doesn’t just come through the eyes,
it also comes through movement, vibration,
sound. And yet there are tons of apps that ignore it completely.
Even though that kind of detail reassures
the user a lot, because they don’t need to think,
they feel that the action went through.
Basically, you need to think of your mobile app as a tool that’s used quickly,
often with one hand and in an imperfect context.
So it needs even less thinking and a lot more feeling.
